label: Auto case 5
classification_reasoning: Requires a specific past example of project leadership under a tight deadline
question: Tell me about a time you led a project under a tight deadline.
response: At FinBridge we had to update an integration for a partner API before they deprecated the old version. I was one of the main engineers on it. The deadline was coming up quickly, maybe a month or so. I helped break down the tasks, implemented some endpoint changes, and coordinated with QA. We shipped it before the deadline. It was stressful, but it worked out.

follow_ups.question: What specifically did you own on that project?
follow_ups.response: I owned the mapping changes for two of the partner response types and wrote some tests for them. I also helped debug a production issue after launch where one optional field was coming through empty. I was not the official lead, but I was responsible for a decent chunk of the work.

label: Auto case 6
classification_reasoning: Requires a past example of persuasion, making it behavioral.
question: Describe a situation where you had to persuade a skeptical stakeholder.
response: There was a time when product wanted a dashboard change faster than engineering thought was safe. I explained that we needed more time to do it properly. I do not remember all the details, but I showed them that rushing it might create rework. Eventually they agreed to give us a bit more time.

follow_ups.question: What evidence did you use to convince them?
follow_ups.response: Mostly examples from previous dashboard bugs. We had a few cases where metrics were confusing because the definitions were not clear. I pulled up one ticket where support had to explain a wrong-looking number to customers. It was not a formal analysis, but it helped make the risk more concrete.

label: Auto case 7
classification_reasoning: Directly prompts for a past example of conflict resolution
question: Tell me about a time you had a conflict with a teammate.
response: I had a disagreement with another engineer about whether to add unit tests for a helper function or just cover it through integration tests. I thought unit tests would be useful, and he thought it was unnecessary. We went back and forth a bit. Eventually we compromised and added a smaller set of unit tests.

follow_ups.question: How did you handle the conflict in the moment?
follow_ups.response: I probably could have handled it better. At first I pushed my point too hard in the pull request comments. After that I messaged him directly and we talked through the concern. It ended fine, but I learned that PR comments can sound sharper than intended.

label: Auto case 8
classification_reasoning: Directly prompts for a past example of failure or mistake
question: Tell me about a time you failed or made a mistake.
response: I once deployed a change that caused a reporting job to miss some records. It was not a huge outage, but it created manual cleanup work. The issue was that I assumed a timestamp field was always present, and it was not. I rolled back the change and added a null check later.

follow_ups.question: What did you change afterward to prevent that kind of issue?
follow_ups.response: I added a test case for missing timestamps and updated the runbook with the symptom. I also started checking edge cases more carefully when dealing with partner data. I would not say it transformed the whole process, but it made me more cautious.

label: Auto case 9

question: Tell me about a time you improved a process.
response: Our deploy process had a few manual steps that people sometimes forgot. I wrote a script to automate part of it. It saved some time and made deployments easier.

follow_ups.question: Can you be more specific about what the script did and how much time it saved?
follow_ups.response: It checked that migrations had run, confirmed the service health endpoint, and posted the deployment version to Slack. Before that, people copied commands from a wiki. It probably saved ten or fifteen minutes per deploy and reduced small mistakes, though I do not have a precise metric.

question: Tell me about a time you mentored someone or helped them grow.
response: I helped a newer engineer get familiar with our codebase. I reviewed their pull requests and answered questions. They got more comfortable over time.

follow_ups.question: What was your actual approach to mentoring them?
follow_ups.response: I set up a weekly thirty-minute pairing session for a few weeks. We walked through one service together, and I asked them to explain changes before opening PRs. I also gave more context in reviews instead of just saying what to change. It helped, but I did not have a formal mentorship plan.

label: Auto case 12
classification_reasoning: Requires a past example of handling competing priorities
question: How do you handle competing priorities when everything is urgent?
response: I try to compare impact and deadlines, then talk to my manager if there is a conflict. I usually ask which item matters most to the business. I am not always perfect at it, but I try not to quietly work late on everything.

follow_ups.question: Can you give me a past example when you had to do that?
follow_ups.response: Earlier this year I had a partner bug, a reporting cleanup task, and an internal tool request all due around the same time. I asked my manager to confirm priority. The partner bug came first because it affected customer data, then the reporting cleanup, and the tool request moved by a week. I fixed the bug in two days and avoided overcommitting.
